item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So there are two links I'm going propose here. First off, the consciousness jumping here reminds me very much of that seen in SCP-1411 that allows the researcher to take over the distant robots, its likely the Foundation may make a connection between one piece of alien tech and another having such similar functions. Additionally, it was mentioned above that the original intention to link this was to SCP-1050, which I feel can work as a finale reference near the end. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

A human subject in contact with SCP-1980-1 will have his/her higher mental functions dominated by those of the entity contained within SCP-1980-1. These subjects are designated SCP-1980-2. The process takes approximately six hours, during which the subject is unconscious. Upon awakening, SCP-1980-1 will have control of the subject, and significant access to the subject’s knowledge and memories. Removal of SCP-1980-1 negates this effect, leaving the subject physically unharmed, but disoriented in a manner resembling the application of amnestics. The degree of disorientation is commensurate with the amount of time exposed to SCP-1980-1.

To this:

A human subject in contact with SCP-1980-1 will have his/her higher mental functions dominated by those of the entity contained within SCP-1980-1. These subjects are designated SCP-1980-2. The process takes approximately six hours, during which the subject is unconscious. Upon awakening, SCP-1980-1 will have control of the subject, and significant access to the subject’s knowledge and memories. Removal of SCP-1980-1 negates this effect, leaving the subject physically unharmed, but disoriented in a manner resembling the application of amnestics. The degree of disorientation is commensurate with the amount of time exposed to SCP-1980-1.1

Changing this:

"One of the last … our bodies may feed the Screamers, but perhaps someone will find our minds."

To this:

"One of the last … our bodies may feed the Screamers, but perhaps someone will find our minds."

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between this technology and that observed in SCP-1411 has been noted and investigation into a potential shared origin between the anomalies is under investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is Yith, but less impressive. I'm just… unmoved. Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There seems to be a bit of the Elder Things in there too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Elder Things had a colony in antarctica. This is like the Great Race of Yith and the Elder Things only…. something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Now that you mention it, maybe 963 was based on these things?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any person believed to have been exposed to SCP-1980-1 is henceforth designated an example of SCP-1980-2, and is to be contained and transported to Site-41 for interrogation and forcible removal of SCP-1980-1.

Now I have the mental image of the O5s wondering why their interrogation reports all read "Lies" and they keep getting requests for more sharp things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's similar to The Thing, but, hey, I liked loved fucking named myself after one of the characters. +1

Should have used the 1982 spot instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would be a lot easier than terraforming. Didn't a movie just (maybe releasing soon?) come out about aliens who take over the human mind and just live in our bodies?

Necro commenting beside, I liked this one, had a nice mystery feeling to it, though the interview at the end reminded me of other SCP logs, I felt this one fit better without being forced. /twocents

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sums up my feelings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this when I saw it in the forums, and I like it now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made me think of Jim Butcher's Denarians; fallen angels imprinted on coins that overcome the minds of anyone who touches them, but lose influence if the coin is removed. That didn't keep me from enjoying this, just wanted to add it to the pile with the Yith, et al.

I particularly like how one could read this in two different ways, both making good stories. On the one hand, everything could be taken at face value. Alien refugees, taking drastic steps to survive any way they can against an unstoppable threat. But then again, all we really have in support of that is the word of an alien body-snatcher. The very first thing they did was infiltrate our staff and make a run for it; who knows what their true goals are? Maybe this is how their species got around the problems of interplanetary colonization. Don't go to all the effort and expense of terraforming (xenoforming?) planets and freighting around settlers, just brain-jack the natives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I edited that part of the Containment Procedures to make it clearer what SCP-1980-2 is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took your advice and cleaned up those sentences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A neutrino telescope is this thing: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IceCube_Neutrino_Observatory

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think the link to SCP-1050 detracted from the overall article. I for one thought that the redactions actually worked pretty well here. The subtlety of it improves the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what confused me. The reference to 1980-2 is brief and is not given its own separate description. Since this is a report documenting and recording the nature of this SCP, i think it should be more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like this more if it were cleaned up a bit. It's sort of messy and confusing. What's SCP-1980-2? I think 1980-1 is the disks… I are confuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very ambitious and quite interesting, but the first thing I was reminded of was the Vault of Souls in the Inheritance Cycle, and I feel the "stored our souls and minds away from a great threat" angle is a bit overplayed in the world of fiction, especially recently. Fix the errors, etc. that other people have pointed out further upthread (I dont wanna repeat everything back to you) and I'll come back for an upvote. Neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, pretty, good….

It might need some more work, I see a lot of loose ends here, also the "evil destroyers" vibe at the end didn't do a lot for me… and I also liked the associations that came with SCP-1980-5. +1, but would love to see the SCP a bit more expanded. Maybe in a tale?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted at brains on CD. I think this is a cool execution of a pretty simple concept. Got some stuff for you to think about :3

Critical Hit!
Attack Missed!

"Any person (SCP-1980-2) believed to have been exposed to SCP-1980-1 is to be contained if possible"
Should it not be "Any person believed to have been exposed to SCP-1980-1 (henceforth SCP-1980-2) is to be contained if possible"? With the parenthesis after person, it just implies that any person is an instance of SCP-1980-2.

"… 14.5 million years ago, by unknown entities, in one …" I don't think you need to specify who created it, especially since it's pretty clear the race is unknown as the article goes on. If you want to keep those three words in the sentence, do it like this; "SCP-1980 was constructed by unknown entities approximately 14.5 million years ago". The fewer commas, the better. Too many commas is why I stopped reading Jack Campbell books.

Personally, I don't like the way point 3 ends. It looks like a thing what is another SCP. Obviously you know what you were going for, but would the Foundation blank out a part of the description if it's another SCP? Surely they'd want people to know what they're dealing with in that respect? Have I missed something? Turns out I did, reading previous comments. Instead of black boxing, specify it's speculation. I think that link, while possibly tenuous, is still important and shouldn't necessarily be hidden.

"when an automated distress signal activated and was intercepted by Foundation personnel"
I'm nosy. I'd like to know why there was a distress signal sent from a seemingly fully operational research facility. It might break the flow, though. Ignore me if so :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is a neutrino telescope? As far as I knew, neutrino detectors are generally massive amounts of matter surrounded by detectors to measure possible particle interactions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I say it right up in the Containment Procedures:

Any person (SCP-1980-2) believed to have been exposed to SCP-1980-1…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless I missed something, you never explicitly define what an SCP-1980-2 is. It's alluded to in a lot of places, but the document should define it as it does all the other variations. I think if this were simplified it would be more successful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assumed they found out from the aliens, but having explicitly mentioned that for a couple other things, didn't want to be redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A telescope, apparently fixed for long-term observation of an area of intergalactic space at coordinates [REDACTED]. No significant astronomic bodies or activity noted in this area.

Did they figure out [REDACTED] by interrogating the alien minds? Or by how the telescope is fixed? If the latter:

1) If the telescope pointing in a fixed direction relative to the surface of the Earth, that direction is going to change some as the Earth rotates (though not by much, since it's only 400km from the south pole), plus change as the Earth's pole precession over tens of thousands of years.

2) Knowing the direction, how did they determine how far away the area of interest is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removed all of the non-critical redaction. It might read a bit cleaner now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's simple because the Foundation has SCP-1980-2 subjects contained, who were willing to tell the Foundation what happened to them and how their artifacts work. They WANT us to use them! I've tried to suggest that the Foundation (or at least the O5s) know more about this SCP than they are letting get into the report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, the expungement here was supposed to be consistent with the expungement in 963. The idea is that the O5s have been trying to replicate SCP-963-1 for years, for some unknown purpose, and failed until finding SCP-1980, which achieves a similar (but not identical) effect in a completely different way, from which they made 963-2. They wanted the 1980/963 link completely out of the SCP-1980 document, but a bit was left in via sloppy in-universe expungement.

The O5s are doing something with this technology, and they don't want anyone to know what it is.

OK, the Great Race of Yith. Yes, being a Lovecraft fan, I know about them. I wasn't really thinking about them when I wrote this, though I can see the similarity of effect now that a few people mentioned it. As best as I can recall, the Great Race can project their minds and take over other beings through time and space, without any special technology. When they do it, the victim's mind is swapped into the body of the Yithian. So I think it falls into "the same class of effects" and not an actual copy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just don't like the idea of how everything is so mysterious here but how we found it is so simple. I think you could of gone more in depth with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tech that built the place either didn't survive the 14.5m years, or is still buried down there. It took substantial work for the Foundation to uncover one structure, knowing exactly where to find it…there could all kinds of other stuff from the alien colonization down there, but I'm assuming we don't know where other interesting things are, and it's beyond the capabilities (and maintenance of secrecy) to melt massive areas of Antarctic ice at random, hoping that there is a whole city down there.

This seems to imply they initially had bodies when they came to earth. If they have suspended animation technology that can work for 14.5 million years why wouldn't they at least keep one working host body available?

They did. The invaders killed all of them. Why they left the lab intact, and left one of their own kind behind, is one of the mysteries of this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1980 is a structure located ████m under the Antarctic ice

Based on the depth at which it was found, SCP-1980 was constructed approximately 14.5 million years ago

There were temperate forests in Antarctica up until 3 million years ago given some variation in ice thickness, and without knowing the original altitude of the site it would not be possible to get an exact depth.

The successful creation of SCP-963-2 shortly after the discovery of SCP-1980 [DATA EXPUNGED]

The initial research team was compromised by SCP-1980-1, so everything would have happened in a relatively short timeframe. SCP-963-2 could have been created after interrogating the original researchers. As far as the expungment it is consistant with the way SCP-963 is written.

On ██/██/████, orders were given by O5-3 to attempt to replicate SCP-963-1. All attempts met with failure until SCP-963-2, at which point [SUBSEQUENT DATA EXPUNGED]

ALL INFORMATION REGARDING SCP-963-2 IS CLASSIFIED LEVEL 5. ANYONE ATTEMPTING TO ACCESS FURTHER INFORMATION ABOUT SCP-963-2 WITHOUT LEVEL 5 CLEARANCE WILL BE TERMINATED.

As far as the Great race of Yith, the idea of mind transfer is pretty common in fiction, it's probably just a coincidence

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1980 is a structure located ████m under the Antarctic ice

But then literally the next sentence,

Based on the depth at which it was found, SCP-1980 was constructed approximately 14.5 million years ago

You know, by that date, you can find out approximately how deep the Skip was. For the sake of 'realism' (for what it's worth on a site like this) the 14.5 really should be marked out. (trust me, the ██.█ would be better then what it is now.)

The successful creation of SCP-963-2 shortly after the discovery of SCP-1980 [DATA EXPUNGED]

What? I thought SCP-963-2 was created after contact with -1's. If you were trying to make suspense with the '[DATA EXPUNGED]', then it failed because I can't even make a theory as to what it is. I'm sure there are a few more typos and over-sites that need fixing (just the nature of writing). But thing like that just bother me. Just my opinion though.

Pretty dull. Then I was reminded of the Great Race of Yith. Not sure if you were just unaware or you based this off of Lovecraft's idea. Whichever it is, I'll have to agree with Djoric. Sorry, but this gets a down-vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may have worded that badly. It doesn't necessarily have to mean literal robots, just some technology capable of building this place. The point is these entities occupying SCP-1980-1 claim to have built this facility, there are records that seem to indicate that seem to support this, if we can trust the entity occupying a D-Class to provide us with an accurate translation. I don't know if the author intended to imply the translation was inaccurate.

Also

70% of us lost…need to find new hosts for the modules.

This seems to imply they initially had bodies when they came to earth. If they have suspended animation technology that can work for 14.5 million years why wouldn't they at least keep one working host body available?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So something physically constructed this place. I don't see any reference to any sort of construction robots.

The methods by which the entities unknown which constructed 1980 are not necessarily known - in fact, I'd be surprised if the Foundation knew. Therefore, the lack of reference to "construction robots" isn't terribly surprising.

What exactly is SCP-1980-5? If it's one of the Destroyers, why is it just chilling in suspended animation. Unless…

The Foundation doesn't know what it is, exactly, or why it's in suspended animation, or whether it's in suspended animation by choice, or if/when it will wake up, or any of these things. Sometimes, the Foundation finds weird anomalous stuff and doesn't know what it is - I personally don't like seeing details being presented when there's no reason for the Foundation to have reasonably learned said details.

Mind you, this is all just my personal interpretation - I wasn't involved in writing this, so eric_h may have other/different answers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, but I have some questions.

Based on the depth at which it was found, SCP-1980 was constructed approximately 14.5 million years ago, by unknown entities

So something physically constructed this place. I don't see any reference to any sort of construction robots.

A biological containment area, containing several primitive hominoids (SCP-1980-4) in suspended animation, as well as an entity (SCP-1980-5) closely resembling the description of a [REDACTED] within SCP-████.

So we have some bodies, several native, one not…

Dr. Martin: Our people. We built the place you found.

"70% of us lost…need to find new hosts for the modules. The local life here is incompatible for transfer. ████████████ tried to merge with a captured [REDACTED]; knew that wouldn't work…they are too different.

It seems it wasn't the natives… What exactly is SCP-1980-5? If it's one of the Destroyers, why is it just chilling in suspended animation. Unless…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. That's a good line, and I like how entirely non-malicious these guys are. There's a lot I like in this. But I don't think it's an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now. Not all of this is great, and I find the last addendum pointless… but…

Dr. Martin: Yes! You could wear your own bodies…you would hardly know the difference.

I utterly love this. Both the concept of separating the body from the mind, then using the body again, and the fact that they don't mind us being saved too, in fact will help if it facilitates cooperation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sort of like Dr Bright. But with aliens. And the ever-mysterious Pattern Screamers…

…Didn't really catch my attention, to be honest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like this. I really do. You set out to create a good example of cross referencing and it shows. If you can make the connections seem less forced then I will happily up-vote. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I need to learn to start tagging my sarcasm. -_-

In all seriousness… this just seems kinda "meh" to me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said, works for me either way. It's just some members of the wiki don't always recognize the subtlety of such things - I knew you understood, based on our previous back-and-forth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not mean "This is literally the Great Race" and more "This is a bunch of dudes who are like the Great Race, except not cool."

Also, Australia separated from Antarctica.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not good to expunge actual procedure — how to contain an object, what to do if it escapes, etc. but in this case it's more "Leave this thing alone; we're not going to tell you why."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't the great race live in Australia, though?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are rare exceptions where it works. Remember why that rule of thumb is there - if I am the poor sod who has to handle a containment breach, I need to know what to [not] do to handle the SCP safely. Telling me "don't do X because it's unsafe because of [Y that is redacted]", well, I know not to do X (since it's stated), and as long as Y simply states the unsafe results of X (and not other things to [not] do), it's not a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reverting the link to blackboxes. It is speculation…they can't know, but someone can say, "Hey, this thing sounds like what was described in 1050!" Maybe it is, and maybe it isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bad redaction all over the place, and Her Most Holy and Revered Empress Utmai Cjen VI of the Third Empire does not approve of these incompetents moving in on her turf.

And I really don't like the Pattern Screamers.

e: Also, this is the Great Race of Yith. Except less cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if there are exceptions, but I don't think you're supposed to expunge data for the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this (and definitely am upvoting it). Lest anyone question, I did give blessing for this to implicitly or explicitly reference 1050.

Don't get me wrong, I like the possibility that the Foundation may have found more information that might have to do with 1050. That said, I have misgivings about the 1050 losing its ambiguity. In other words, in 1050 I try and clearly demarcate what the Foundation knows (fact) and what the Foundation thinks (speculation). If something comes out and attempts to definitively say "hey, this definitely [dis]proves this bit of speculation about 1050", I'm going to oppose it. So, I encourage making the apparent link a little more speculative - the Foundation can't know definitively that 1980-5 is anything like 1050's Destroyers. I'm not sure how to go about doing that, but that's my thoughts.

Also, I do not mind (or think it would detract) if you redact "1050" to the blackbox state. Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your current change works pretty well, but I also like just "Not compatible for transfer/melding/whatever-you-want-to-call-it." I was thinking it would be more ambiguous and leave readers to decide why we humans are compatible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A biological containment area, containing several primitive hominoids (SCP-1980-4) in suspended animation, as well as an entity (SCP-1980-5) closely resembling the description of a [REDACTED] within SCP-1050.

Never cross-reference an SCP which is better than yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might also be better if you removed the cross-link to SCP-1050; the connection feels a bit forced, and it can be up to the reader to decide if the two are connected.

Originally, I had it blackboxed as SCP-████, but realized in chat that if you hadn't read 1050, you might be really confused. So I made it explicit. I actually liked it better the other way, but there is a lot going on in this SCP, so have been editing for clarity.

not nearly evolved enough. Also, this is one of my pet peeves. :/

OK, how should I say it? The idea is that 14.5m years ago, there were no humans, but (I believe) something that ended up being the ancestor of both apes and humans. They were not smart enough to be hosts for SCP-1980-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the cross-linking to SCP-1050 which is one of my favorite of all time…but I must agree that this attempt is more an outline for a story/tale then an SCP.
I would love to see this re-worked, though.
No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Man is the warmest place to hide.";)

I dunno. I like the concept, and I like some of the execution, but the last addendum seems completely unnecessary, and just muddles up the basic concept (Some aliens left their brains on discs that automatically download their personalities into people, and they did this to escape something worse).

It might also be better if you removed the cross-link to SCP-1050; the connection feels a bit forced, and it can be up to the reader to decide if the two are connected.

not nearly evolved enough.
Also, this is one of my pet peeves. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a metallic disc with crystals that stores a conscious mind and takes over any individual it comes in contact with. Is the resemblance to SCP-963 intentional?

Edited due to missed struck out text

The successful creation of SCP-963-2 shortly after the discovery of SCP-1980 [DATA EXPUNGED]. It does not appear to be possible to recreate SCP-1980-3 or SCP-1980-1 templates using current human technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. If it were, say, 1550m down, I thought 1.55km would look weird, especially when redacted as █.██ km.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because the structure isn't multiple kilometers beneath the ice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
████m under the Antarctic ice, approximately 400 km from the South Pole

Why wouldn't both measurements be in km?
